semantic_journey: Arabic قنطار (qintar) = a unit of weight (100 ratls); itself from Latin centenarium via Aramaic. Greek κεντηνάριον = 100 librae, also from Latin centenarium. Both Arabic qintar and Greek kentenarion are loanwords from the SAME Latin source word (centenarium). This is a shared loanword from Latin, not an Arabic-to-Greek cognate.
key_insight: Both Arabic qintar and Greek kentenarion derive from Latin centenarium; this is Latin transmitted into two languages separately, not Arabic-to-Greek.

semantic_journey: Arabic اثم (ithm) = sin, moral transgression, guilt — what is morally forbidden. Greek Θέμις = divine law, right order, sacred custom — what is divinely ordained. These are two sides of the same moral-legal concept: ithm is the VIOLATION, Themis is the LAW being violated. Arabic moral-transgression → Greek divine-law is a negation-cognate relationship: the Arabic word defines the wrong act, the Greek word defines the right order that was broken.
key_insight: Arabic sin/transgression (ithm) and Greek divine law (Themis) are the two sides of the same moral-legal concept: violation vs. the law violated.

semantic_journey: Arabic خضر (khudr/akhdar) = green, verdant, fresh, unripe (specifically the fresh green state before ripening). Greek χῖδρον = unripe grain, parched unripe barley grains. Both encode the unripe/fresh green state of grain. Arabic green-unripe → Greek unripe-grain is a direct 1-step specialization: Arabic covers the general fresh/green state, Greek names the specific unripe grain product.
key_insight: Arabic green/unripe (khudr) directly matches Greek unripe grain (khidron) — the freshness/unripeness concept preserved in grain terminology.

semantic_journey: Arabic القشنيزه (al-qushnaiza) = coriander (Coriandrum sativum), a culinary herb. Greek γάλαξιν = related to γάλα (milk); possibly referring to a milky plant or the galaxy. The reasoning 'eaten by people and loved by sheep' connects only at the broadest agricultural level. Coriander and milk/milky-plants are entirely different botanical categories. No meaningful semantic path.
key_insight: Coriander and Greek milk-related term share only the loosest pastoral association; completely different botanical and semantic categories.

semantic_journey: Arabic سرنج (saranj) = a form of crafted mosaic or inlaid work; intricate patterned material. Greek σαργάνη = plaited/braided rope, a woven cord or basket made by plaiting. Both encode intricate material patterning through repeated inter-weaving. Arabic inlaid-pattern craft → Greek plaited/braided work is a 1-step parallel: both describe structured interlacing to create a composite material.
key_insight: Arabic inlaid mosaic-work (saranj) and Greek plaited rope/basket (sargane) share the conceptual core of structured interlacing.

semantic_journey: Arabic القعس (al-qas) = bent backward, arched shape (specifically the backward arch of the spine or a bow). Greek ἕλιξ = spiral, coil, anything curved or twisted. Both encode a specific curved/arched geometric form. Arabic backward-arch → Greek spiral/coil is the same 1-step generalization pattern seen in GRC_0159 and GRC_0182: Arabic names a specific arc, Greek generalizes to any coil/curve.
key_insight: Arabic backward-arch form (qaas) and Greek spiral/coil (helix) share the geometric core of curved form.

semantic_journey: Arabic دفيء (dafi) = warmth, being warm, gentle warmth. Greek δέφω = to soften by warmth, to knead, to work leather or bread dough by warming. Both encode warmth-based softening. Arabic warmth → Greek soften-by-warmth is a direct 1-step connection: dafi is the heat, depho is what heat does to material (softens it).
key_insight: Arabic warmth (dafi) and Greek soften-by-warmth/knead (depho) share the core of gentle heat acting on material.

semantic_journey: Arabic دخن (dukhan) = smoke; to smoke, emit smoke. Greek διαχύνω = to pour out through, diffuse, spread widely. Both encode the diffusing/spreading action of something that moves through space. Arabic smoke spreading/diffusing → Greek diffusing/spreading outward is a 1-step parallel: smoke is the canonical example of something that pours and diffuses through the air.
key_insight: Arabic smoke (dukhan) spreading through air maps directly to Greek diffusing/pouring-through (diakhy) — smoke as the model of diffusion.
